|doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{citation needed|Kin-gū}} [[en:Kin-gū]] {{Shinto side}} == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}.[[ja:金武宮]] All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[en:Kin-gū]][[sn:Kin-gū]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kin-gū}} [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]] {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} [[en:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[en:Kin-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kin-gū}}[[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]]

[https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}.[[ja:金武宮]] All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[en:Kin-gū]][[sn:Kin-gū]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kin-gū}} [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]] {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} [[en:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[en:Kin-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kin-gū}}[[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}} | image = [[File:Kin-gū.JPG|270px|{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}'s altar]]
{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}'s altar. Left: Senju Kannon. | location = 222 Kin, [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 27 | type seconds = 19.1 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 55 | longitude seconds = 18.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Okinawa Island | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = During the reign of King Shō Sei (1527 - 1555) | main hall = [[Hokora]] | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} [[File:KinKannonji.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kin Kannon-ji Temple
The entrance to the cave where {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}} is located is to the right in the image.]] [[File:Entry of Kin cave.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Cave entrance
Located on the east side of the shrine grounds.]] '''{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}''' (金武宮, Kin-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Meiji period]]. == Deities == ; Kumano Gongen * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] * [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] == History == The exact origin of the shrine is unknown. According to ''Shrines of Okinawa'', no shrine buildings were constructed since its founding''Shrines of Okinawa'', 2000, p. 32.. The ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Kinpusan Kannon-ji, contains the "Kimpusan Fudarakuin Kannon-ji Temple Origin," which mentions that the monk [[Nisshu (Shingon)|Nisshu Shonin]], who traveled in search of Mount Fudaraku, founded the temple during the reign of King Shō Sei. He identified the cave at this location as a sacred site and built the shrine, enshrining three deities he carved himself. According to ''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', the stalactite cave is considered the main body of the shrine. Therefore, it is believed that Nisshu Shonin, who established Kannon-ji Temple, also founded this shrine. This would date the shrine's founding to between 1527 and 1555, the reign of King Shō Sei. Under the "official shrine system," it was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines. As the shrine did not have a resident priest and was managed by the abbot of Kannon-ji Temple, it did not receive financial support from the royal government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{citation needed|Kin-gū}} [[en:Kin-gū]] {{Shinto side}} == Content taken from by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}.[[ja:金武宮]] All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[en:Kin-gū]][[sn:Kin-gū]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kin-gū}} [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]] {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} [[en:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[en:Kin-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kin-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]]

[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = During the reign of King Shō Sei (1527 - 1555) | main hall = [[Hokora]] | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} [[File:KinKannonji.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kin Kannon-ji Temple
The entrance to the cave where {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}} is located is to the right in the image.]] [[File:Entry of Kin cave.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Cave entrance
Located on the east side of the shrine grounds.]] '''{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}''' (金武宮, Kin-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Meiji period]]. == Deities == ; Kumano Gongen * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] * [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] == History == The exact origin of the shrine is unknown. According to ''Shrines of Okinawa'', no shrine buildings were constructed since its founding''Shrines of Okinawa'', 2000, p. 32.. The ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Kinpusan Kannon-ji, contains the "Kimpusan Fudarakuin Kannon-ji Temple Origin," which mentions that the monk [[Nisshu (Shingon)|Nisshu Shonin]], who traveled in search of Mount Fudaraku, founded the temple during the reign of King Shō Sei. He identified the cave at this location as a sacred site and built the shrine, enshrining three deities he carved himself. According to ''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', the stalactite cave is considered the main body of the shrine. Therefore, it is believed that Nisshu Shonin, who established Kannon-ji Temple, also founded this shrine. This would date the shrine's founding to between 1527 and 1555, the reign of King Shō Sei. Under the "official shrine system," it was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines. As the shrine did not have a resident priest and was managed by the abbot of Kannon-ji Temple, it did not receive financial support from the royal government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{citation needed|Kin-gū}} [[en:Kin-gū]] {{Shinto side}} == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}.[[ja:金武宮]] All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[en:Kin-gū]][[sn:Kin-gū]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kin-gū}} [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]] {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} [[en:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[en:Kin-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kin-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]]

the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}.[[ja:金武宮]] All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[en:Kin-gū]][[sn:Kin-gū]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kin-gū}} [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]] {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} [[en:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[en:Kin-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kin-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[sn:Kin-gū]][[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}} | image = [[File:Kin-gū.JPG|270px|{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}'s altar]]
{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}'s altar. Left: Senju Kannon. | location = 222 Kin, [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 27 | type seconds = 19.1 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 55 | longitude seconds = 18.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Okinawa Island | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = During the reign of King Shō Sei (1527 - 1555) | main hall = [[Hokora]] | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} [[File:KinKannonji.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kin Kannon-ji Temple
The entrance to the cave where {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}} is located is to the right in the image.]] [[File:Entry of Kin cave.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Cave entrance
Located on the east side of the shrine grounds.]] '''{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}''' (金武宮, Kin-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Meiji period]]. == Deities == ; Kumano Gongen * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] * [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] == History == The exact origin of the shrine is unknown. According to ''Shrines of Okinawa'', no shrine buildings were constructed since its founding''Shrines of Okinawa'', 2000, p. 32.. The ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Kinpusan Kannon-ji, contains the "Kimpusan Fudarakuin Kannon-ji Temple Origin," which mentions that the monk [[Nisshu (Shingon)|Nisshu Shonin]], who traveled in search of Mount Fudaraku, founded the temple during the reign of King Shō Sei. He identified the cave at this location as a sacred site and built the shrine, enshrining three deities he carved himself. According to ''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', the stalactite cave is considered the [[Shintai|main body]] of the shrine. Therefore, it is believed that Nisshu Shonin, who established Kannon-ji Temple, also founded this shrine. This would date the shrine's founding to between 1527 and 1555, the reign of King Shō Sei. Under the "official shrine system," it was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines. As the shrine did not have a resident priest and was managed by the abbot of Kannon-ji Temple, it did not receive financial support from the royal government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}.[[ja:金武宮]] All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{citation needed|Kin-gū}} [[en:Kin-gū]] {{Shinto side}} [[en:Kin-gū]][[sn:Kin-gū]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kin-gū}} [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]] {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} [[en:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[en:Kin-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kin-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[sn:Kin-gū]][[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]]

The entrance to the cave where {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}} is located is to the right in the image.]] [[File:Entry of Kin cave.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Cave entrance
Located on the east side of the shrine grounds.]] '''{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}}''' (金武宮, Kin-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Meiji period]]. == Deities == ; Kumano Gongen * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] * [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] == History == The exact origin of the shrine is unknown. According to ''Shrines of Okinawa'', no shrine buildings were constructed since its founding''Shrines of Okinawa'', 2000, p. 32.. The ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Kinpusan Kannon-ji, contains the "Kimpusan Fudarakuin Kannon-ji Temple Origin," which mentions that the monk [[Nisshu (Shingon)|Nisshu Shonin]], who traveled in search of Mount Fudaraku, founded the temple during the reign of King Shō Sei. He identified the cave at this location as a sacred site and built the shrine, enshrining three deities he carved himself. According to ''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', the stalactite cave is considered the [[Shintai|main body]] of the shrine. Therefore, it is believed that Nisshu Shonin, who established Kannon-ji Temple, also founded this shrine. This would date the shrine's founding to between 1527 and 1555, the reign of King Shō Sei. Under the "official shrine system," it was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines. As the shrine did not have a resident priest and was managed by the abbot of Kannon-ji Temple, it did not receive financial support from the royal government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|12=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|12=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|12=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|12=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|12=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{citation needed|Kin-gū}} {{Shinto side}} {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[ja:金武宮]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kin-gū}}

However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{citation needed|Kin-gū}} {{Shinto side}} {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[ja:金武宮]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kin-gū}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}} | image = [[File:Kin-gū.JPG|270px|{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}}'s altar]]
{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}}'s altar. Left: Senju Kannon. | location = 222 Kin, [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 27 | type seconds = 19.1 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 55 | longitude seconds = 18.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Okinawa Island | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine Rank = Former unRanked shrine | founded = During the reign of King Shō Sei (1527 - 1555) | main hall = [[Hokora]] | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} [[File:KinKannonji.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kin Kannon-ji Temple
The entrance to the cave where {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}} is located is to the right in the image.]] [[File:Entry of Kin cave.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Cave entrance
Located on the east side of the shrine grounds.]] '''{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}}''' (金武宮, Kin-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was an unRanked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Meiji period]]. == Deities == ; Kumano Gongen * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] * [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] == History == The exact origin of the shrine is unknown. According to ''Shrines of Okinawa'', no shrine buildings were constructed since its founding''Shrines of Okinawa'', 2000, p. 32.. The ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Kinpusan Kannon-ji, contains the "Kimpusan Fudarakuin Kannon-ji Temple Origin," which mentions that the monk [[Nisshu (Shingon)|Nisshu Shonin]], who traveled in search of Mount Fudaraku, founded the temple during the reign of King Shō Sei. He identified the cave at this location as a sacred site and built the shrine, enshrining three deities he carved himself. According to ''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', the stalactite cave is considered the [[Shintai|main body]] of the shrine. Therefore, it is believed that Nisshu Shonin, who established Kannon-ji Temple, also founded this shrine. This would date the shrine's founding to between 1527 and 1555, the reign of King Shō Sei. Under the "official shrine system," it was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines. As the shrine did not have a resident priest and was managed by the abbot of Kannon-ji Temple, it did not receive financial support from the royal government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|12=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|12=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|12=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|12=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|12=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unRanked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{citation needed|Kin-gū}} {{Shinto side}} {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kin-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kin-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:UnRanked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[ja:金武宮]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]] {{citation needed|Kin-gū}} [[Category:Ambox transclusions]]

According to ''Shrines of Okinawa'', no shrine buildings were constructed since its founding''Shrines of Okinawa'', 2000, p. 32.. The ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Kinpusan Kannon-ji, contains the "Kimpusan Fudarakuin Kannon-ji Temple Origin," which mentions that the monk [[Nisshu (Shingon)|Nisshu Shonin]], who traveled in search of Mount Fudaraku, founded the temple during the reign of King Shō Sei. He identified the cave at this location as a sacred site and built the shrine, enshrining three deities he carved himself. According to ''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', the stalactite cave is considered the [[Shintai|main body]] of the shrine. Therefore, it is believed that Nisshu Shonin, who established Kannon-ji Temple, also founded this shrine. This would date the shrine's founding to between 1527 and 1555, the reign of King Shō Sei. Under the "official shrine system," it was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines. As the shrine did not have a resident priest and was managed by the abbot of Kannon-ji Temple, it did not receive financial support from the royal government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{citation needed|Kin-gū}} {{Shinto side}} {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kin-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kin-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:UnRanked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[ja:金武宮]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]] {{citation needed|Kin-gū}} [[Category:Ambox transclusions]]

revision.comment: 22 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:金武宮 up to rev 94116751
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 金武宮 |画像 = [[ファイル:Kin-gū.JPG|270px|金武宮の祠]]
右奥が金武宮の祠。左は千手観音。 |所在地 = [[沖縄県]][[国頭郡]][[金武町]]金武222 | 緯度度 = 26 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 19.1 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 127 |経度分 = 55 | 経度秒 = 18.5 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP |地図 = 沖縄本島 |祭神 = [[伊弉冉尊]]
[[速玉男尊]]
[[事解男尊]] |社格 = 旧無格社 |創建 = 尚清王 御代（1527年 - 1555年） |本殿 = [[祠]] |別名 = |札所等 = 琉球八社 |例祭 = |神事 = }} [[ファイル:KinKannonji.JPG|250px|right|thumb|金武観音寺
金武宮の鎮座する鍾乳洞入口は、画像の右手方向にある。]] [[ファイル:Entry of Kin cave.JPG|250px|right|thumb|鍾乳洞入口
境内東側にある。]] '''金武宮'''（きんぐう）は、[[沖縄県]][[国頭郡]][[金武町]]にある[[神社]]。[[琉球八社]]の一つで、[[明治]]の[[近代社格制度]]では[[無格社]]。 == 祭神 == ; 熊野権現 * [[伊弉冉尊]] * [[速玉男命]] * [[事解男命]] == 由緒 == その創始は詳らかでないが、『沖縄の神社』によれば社殿は創建以来設けられていなかった『沖縄の神社』2000年 p32より。。 『[[琉球国由来記]] 巻11 密門諸寺縁起』『琉球史料叢書 第1』1962年に所収。の〔金峰山観音寺〕の段にある「金峰山補陀落院観音寺縁起」には観音寺の開基について記してあるが、それによれば[[補陀落|補陀落山]]を求めて渡来した[[日秀 (真言宗)|日秀上人]]が、[[尚清王]]の御代の[[嘉靖|嘉靖年間]]に[[琉球王国]]内を行脚し、この地にあった洞窟を霊跡として宮を建て、自身が彫った三尊を権現正体として崇め奉ったとしている。 『琉球宗教史の研究』では鍾乳洞が当社の御本体となっているところから推測すれば、観音寺を建立した日秀上人が当社を創立したと見るべきであろうと述べている『琉球宗教史の研究 第5編 明治の宗教政策 第1章 古琉球の社寺概観』より。『琉球宗教史の研究』1965年。が、そうであるなら『琉球国由来記』の記述から当社創建の時期は尚清王が在位した[[1527年]]から[[1555年]]の間ということになる。 当社は「官社の制」により琉球八社の一つとされた。『琉球宗教史の研究』によれば、琉球八社へは神職の役俸並びに営繕費が王府から支給されていたが、当社は神職を置かず観音寺の住職が経営していたため王府からの経済的援助は受けていなかった『琉球宗教史の研究 第5編 明治の宗教政策 第2章 社寺禄の処分 第3節 社禄の処分（神社）』より。『琉球宗教史の研究』1965年。。 [[明治]]に入り、[[沖縄県の歴史#琉球処分|琉球処分]]によって琉球王国が廃され沖縄県が置かれると、明治政府は琉球在来の宗教を弾圧し或いは変革することによって、日本の国家理念の下につくられた日本宗教に移行せしめ、その宗教を通して、琉球人を日本国家および日本精神の下に統合するという政策方針をとった。このため沖縄県行政府では、社殿を持たない特殊な形態の当社も村社に列することを立案したが、従来より氏子組織を持たないため経済的に村社列格が出来ず、また社殿その他の設備においても不備な点が多々あって村社列格が事実上不可能であることから、とりあえず無格社として独立の神社と認められることになった。なお以後については、追々維持拡張整備して村社に引き直す根基を充実するよう努めることとされた『琉球宗教史の研究 第5編 明治の宗教政策 第4章 社寺組織の改革 第2節 神社組織の改革』より。『琉球宗教史の研究』1965年。。 [[昭和]]14年（[[1939年]]）沖縄県振興事業の一部として琉球八社の復興計画が取り上げられた。この時の記録によれば、当社は縦1[[尺]]5[[寸]]（約45[[センチメートル|cm]]）、横2尺（約60cm）、棟高2尺5寸（約76cm）の素焼きの陶製であった『琉球宗教史の研究 第5編 明治の宗教政策 第5章 神社の列格並に創立 第1節 琉球八社』より。『琉球宗教史の研究』1965年。が、現在はテンプレート画像の祠となっている。 {{clear}} == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor1-first=|editor1-last=伊波 普猷|editor1-link=伊波普猷|editor2-first=|editor2-last=東恩納 寛惇|editor2-link=東恩納寛惇|editor3-first=|editor3-last=横山 重|editor3-link=横山重|title=琉球史料叢書 第1|series=琉球史料叢書|publisher=井上書房|year=1962}} ‐ 『琉球国由来記』巻1から巻11を所収。 * {{Cite book|和書|author=鳥越憲三郎 |title=琉球宗教史の研究 |publisher=角川書店 |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=加治順人 |title=沖縄の神社 |publisher=ひるぎ社 |date=2000 |series=おきなわ文庫 |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|和書|author=加治順人 |title=沖縄の神社、その歴史と独自性 |journal=非文字資料研究 |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=神奈川大学日本常民文化研究所非文字資料研究センター |date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == 関連リンク == * {{Cite journal|和書|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=髙橋康夫 |title=補陀落渡海僧日秀上人と琉球 : 史書が創った日秀伝説 |journal=沖縄文化研究= 沖縄文化研究 |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=法政大学沖縄文化研究所 |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} * [[琉球神道]] {{DEFAULTSORT:きんくう}} [[Category:沖縄県の神社]] [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:熊野信仰]] [[Category:沖縄県の旧無格社]] [[Category:金武町の建築物]] [[Category:金武町の歴史]] {{琉球八社}} {{神道 横}}

journal|和書|author=加治順人 |title=沖縄の神社、その歴史と独自性 |journal=非文字資料研究 |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=神奈川大学日本常民文化研究所非文字資料研究センター |date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=法政大学沖縄文化研究所 |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} * [[琉球神道]] {{DEFAULTSORT:きんくう}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}} | image = [[File:Kin-gū.JPG|270px|{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}}'s altar]]
{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}}'s altar. Left: Senju Kannon. | location = 222 Kin, [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 27 | type seconds = 19.1 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 55 | longitude seconds = 18.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Okinawa Island | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine Rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = During the reign of King Shō Sei (1527 - 1555) | main hall = [[Hokora]] | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} [[File:KinKannonji.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kin Kannon-ji Temple
The entrance to the cave where {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}} is located is to the right in the image.]] [[File:Entry of Kin cave.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Cave entrance
Located on the east side of the shrine grounds.]] '''{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}}''' (金武宮, Kin-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Meiji period]]. == Deities == ; Kumano Gongen * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] * [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] == History == The exact origin of the shrine is unknown. According to ''Shrines of Okinawa'', no shrine buildings were constructed since its founding''Shrines of Okinawa'', 2000, p. 32.. The ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Kinpusan Kannon-ji, contains the "Kimpusan Fudarakuin Kannon-ji Temple Origin," which mentions that the monk [[Nisshu (Shingon)|Nisshu Shonin]], who traveled in search of Mount Fudaraku, founded the temple during the reign of King Shō Sei. He identified the cave at this location as a sacred site and built the shrine, enshrining three deities he carved himself. According to ''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', the stalactite cave is considered the [[Shintai|main body]] of the shrine. Therefore, it is believed that Nisshu Shonin, who established Kannon-ji Temple, also founded this shrine. This would date the shrine's founding to between 1527 and 1555, the reign of King Shō Sei. Under the "official shrine system," it was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines. As the shrine did not have a resident priest and was managed by the abbot of Kannon-ji Temple, it did not receive financial support from the royal government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|12=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|12=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|12=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|12=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|12=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|qq=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|qq=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|qq=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965..
