In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1625|qq=}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New|qq=}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Shōken|qq=}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naminoue Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū‎|qq=}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == See Also == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] == Footnotes == == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Shinto side}} {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|金武宮|version=94116751|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[ja:金武宮]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}} | image = [[File:Kin-gū.JPG|270px|{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}'s altar]]
{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}'s altar. Left: Senju Kannon. | location = 222 Kin, [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 27 | type seconds = 19.1 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 55 | longitude seconds = 18.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Okinawa Island | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = During the reign of King Shō Sei (1527 - 1555) | main hall = [[Hokora]] | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} [[File:KinKannonji.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kin Kannon-ji Temple
The entrance to the cave where {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}} is located is to the right in the image.]] [[File:Entry of Kin cave.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Cave entrance
Located on the east side of the shrine grounds.]] '''{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}''' (金武宮, Kin-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}https://www.goteamjosh.com/blog/tag/Eight+Shrines+of+Okinawa and was an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Meiji period]]. == Deities == ; Kumano Gongen * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] == History == The exact origin of the shrine is unknown. According to ''Shrines of Okinawa'', no shrine buildings were constructed since its founding''Shrines of Okinawa'', 2000, p. 32.. The ''[[Ryūkyū-koku yurai-ki|Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Kinpusan Kannon-ji, contains the "Kimpusan Fudarakuin Kannon-ji Temple Origin," which mentions that the monk {{ill|Nisshu (Shingon)|ja|日秀 (真言宗)|lt=Nisshu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nisshu (Shingon)|qq=}}, who traveled in search of Mount Fudaraku, founded the temple during the reign of King Shō Sei. He identified the cave at this location as a sacred site and built the shrine, enshrining three deities he carved himself. According to ''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', the stalactite cave is considered the [[shintai]] of the shrine. Therefore, it is believed that Nisshu Shonin, who established Kannon-ji Temple, also founded this shrine. This would date the shrine's founding to between 1527 and 1555, the reign of King Shō Sei. Under the "official shrine system," it was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines. As the shrine did not have a resident priest and was managed by the abbot of Kannon-ji Temple, it did not receive financial support from the royal government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Invasion of Ryukyu|qq=}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikoe-ōgimi|qq=}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1625|qq=}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New|qq=}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Shōken|qq=}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naminoue Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū‎|qq=}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == Footnotes == == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Shinto side}} {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|金武宮|version=94116751|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[ja:金武宮]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]]

shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New|qq=}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Shōken|qq=}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naminoue Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū‎|qq=}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == Footnotes == == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Shinto side}} {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|金武宮|version=94116751|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[ja:金武宮]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}} | image = [[File:Kin-gū.JPG|270px|{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}'s altar]]
{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}'s altar. Left: Senju Kannon. | location = 222 Kin, [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 27 | type seconds = 19.1 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 55 | longitude seconds = 18.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Okinawa Island | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = During the reign of King Shō Sei (1527 - 1555) | main hall = [[Hokora]] | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} [[File:KinKannonji.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kin Kannon-ji Temple
The entrance to the cave where {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}} is located is to the right in the image.]] [[File:Entry of Kin cave.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Cave entrance
Located on the east side of the shrine grounds.]] '''{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}''' (金武宮, Kin-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}https://www.goteamjosh.com/blog/tag/Eight+Shrines+of+Okinawa and was an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Meiji period]]. == Deities == ; Kumano Gongen * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] == History == The exact origin of the shrine is unknown. According to ''Shrines of Okinawa'', no shrine buildings were constructed since its founding''Shrines of Okinawa'', 2000, p. 32.. The ''[[Ryūkyū-koku yurai-ki|Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Kinpusan Kannon-ji, contains the "Kimpusan Fudarakuin Kannon-ji Temple Origin," which mentions that the monk {{ill|Nisshu (Shingon)|ja|日秀 (真言宗)|lt=Nisshu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nisshu (Shingon)|qq=}}, who traveled in search of Mount Fudaraku, founded the temple during the reign of King Shō Sei. He identified the cave at this location as a sacred site and built the shrine, enshrining three deities he carved himself. According to ''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', the stalactite cave is considered the [[shintai]] of the shrine. Therefore, it is believed that Nisshu Shonin, who established Kannon-ji Temple, also founded this shrine. This would date the shrine's founding to between 1527 and 1555, the reign of King Shō Sei. Under the "official shrine system," it was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines. As the shrine did not have a resident priest and was managed by the abbot of Kannon-ji Temple, it did not receive financial support from the royal government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Invasion of Ryukyu|qq=}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikoe-ōgimi|qq=}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1625|qq=}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New|qq=}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Shōken|qq=}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naminoue Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū‎|qq=}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == Footnotes == == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Shinto side}} {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|金武宮|version=94116751|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[ja:金武宮]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]]

As the shrine did not have a resident priest and was managed by the abbot of Kannon-ji Temple, it did not receive financial support from the royal government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == Footnotes == == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Shinto side}} {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|金武宮|version=94116751|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[ja:金武宮]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]]

Left: Senju Kannon. | location = 222 Kin, [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 27 | type seconds = 19.1 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 55 | longitude seconds = 18.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Okinawa Island | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = During the reign of King Shō Sei (1527 - 1555) | main hall = [[Hokora]] | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} [[File:KinKannonji.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kin Kannon-ji Temple
The entrance to the cave where {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}} is located is to the right in the image.]] [[File:Entry of Kin cave.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Cave entrance
Located on the east side of the shrine grounds.]] '''{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}''' (金武宮, Kin-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}https://www.goteamjosh.com/blog/tag/Eight+Shrines+of+Okinawa and was an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Meiji period]]. == Deities == ; Kumano Gongen * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] == History == The exact origin of the shrine is unknown. According to ''Shrines of Okinawa'', no shrine buildings were constructed since its founding''Shrines of Okinawa'', 2000, p. 32.. The ''[[Ryūkyū-koku yurai-ki|Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Kinpusan Kannon-ji, contains the "Kimpusan Fudarakuin Kannon-ji Temple Origin," which mentions that the monk {{ill|Nisshu (Shingon)|ja|日秀 (真言宗)|lt=Nisshu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nisshu (Shingon)|qq=}}, who traveled in search of Mount Fudaraku, founded the temple during the reign of King Shō Sei. He identified the cave at this location as a sacred site and built the shrine, enshrining three deities he carved himself. According to ''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', the stalactite cave is considered the [[shintai]] of the shrine. Therefore, it is believed that Nisshu Shonin, who established Kannon-ji Temple, also founded this shrine. This would date the shrine's founding to between 1527 and 1555, the reign of King Shō Sei. Under the "official shrine system," it was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines. As the shrine did not have a resident priest and was managed by the abbot of Kannon-ji Temple, it did not receive financial support from the royal government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == Footnotes == == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Shinto side}} {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|金武宮|version=94116751|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[ja:金武宮]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]]

As the shrine did not have a resident priest and was managed by the abbot of Kannon-ji Temple, it did not receive financial support from the royal government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == Footnotes == == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Shinto side}} {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|金武宮|version=94116751|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[ja:金武宮]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:金武町の建築物]]

{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}'s altar. Left: Senju Kannon. | location = 222 Kin, [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 27 | type seconds = 19.1 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 55 | longitude seconds = 18.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Okinawa Island | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = During the reign of King Shō Sei (1527 - 1555) | main hall = [[Hokora]] | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} [[File:KinKannonji.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Kin Kannon-ji Temple
The entrance to the cave where {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}} is located is to the right in the image.]] [[File:Entry of Kin cave.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Cave entrance
Located on the east side of the shrine grounds.]] '''{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}''' (金武宮, Kin-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kin Town]], [[Kunigami District]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|ja|琉球八社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}}https://www.goteamjosh.com/blog/tag/Eight+Shrines+of+Okinawa and was an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Meiji period]]. == Deities == ; Kumano Gongen * [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]] * [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] == History == The exact origin of the shrine is unknown. According to ''Shrines of Okinawa'', no shrine buildings were constructed since its founding''Shrines of Okinawa'', 2000, p. 32.. The ''[[Ryūkyū-koku yurai-ki|Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Kinpusan Kannon-ji, contains the "Kimpusan Fudarakuin Kannon-ji Temple Origin," which mentions that the monk {{ill|Nisshu (Shingon)|ja|日秀 (真言宗)|lt=Nisshu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nisshu (Shingon)|qq=}}, who traveled in search of Mount Fudaraku, founded the temple during the reign of King Shō Sei. He identified the cave at this location as a sacred site and built the shrine, enshrining three deities he carved himself. According to ''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', the stalactite cave is considered the [[shintai]] of the shrine. Therefore, it is believed that Nisshu Shonin, who established Kannon-ji Temple, also founded this shrine. This would date the shrine's founding to between 1527 and 1555, the reign of King Shō Sei. Under the "official shrine system," it was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines. As the shrine did not have a resident priest and was managed by the abbot of Kannon-ji Temple, it did not receive financial support from the royal government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == Footnotes == == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Shinto side}} {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|金武宮|version=94116751|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[ja:金武宮]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:金武町の建築物]] [[Category:金武町の歴史]]

The ''[[Ryūkyū-koku yurai-ki|Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Kinpusan Kannon-ji, contains the "Kimpusan Fudarakuin Kannon-ji Temple Origin," which mentions that the monk {{ill|Nisshu (Shingon)|ja|日秀 (真言宗)|lt=Nisshu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nisshu (Shingon)|qq=}}, who traveled in search of Mount Fudaraku, founded the temple during the reign of King Shō Sei. As the shrine did not have a resident priest and was managed by the abbot of Kannon-ji Temple, it did not receive financial support from the royal government''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} In the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the Meiji government aimed to integrate the Ryukyu people under Japanese state religion by transforming or suppressing native Ryukyu religious practices''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Despite plans to classify the shrine as a village shrine, it remained unranked due to its lack of parishioner organization and inadequate facilities. Efforts were made to gradually improve the shrine for future reclassification as a village shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented. At that time, the shrine was a simple earthenware structure, 1.5 shaku (about 45 cm) wide and 2 shaku (about 60 cm) deep, with a ridge height of 2.5 shaku (about 76 cm)''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current shrine is depicted in the template image. {{clear}} == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] == Footnotes == == References == {{References}} {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe |title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History |publisher=Kadokawa Shoten |date=1965 |NCID=BN07252674 |doi=10.11501/2980004 |id={{NDLJP|2980004}} |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2980004 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa |publisher=Hirugi-sha |date=2000 |series=Okinawa Library |issue=92 |NCID=BA49757948 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite journal|author=Junhito Kaji |title=Shrines of Okinawa: Their History and Uniqueness |journal=Non-Textual Materials Research |ISSN=2432-5481 |publisher=Kanagawa University Institute for Non-Textual Materials|date=2018-09 |issue=16 |pages=37-68 |naid=120006734134 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10487/00015760}} == External Links == * {{Cite journal|url=https://doi.org/10.15002/00007282 |author=Yasuo Takahashi |title=Nisshu Shonin and Ryukyu: The Legend Created by Historical Records |journal=Okinawa Cultural Research |ISSN=1349-4015 |publisher=Hosei University Okinawa Cultural Research Institute |date=2011-03 |issue=37 |pages=1-40 |naid=120003142298 |doi=10.15002/00007282}} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Shinto side}} {{commonscat|Kin-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|金武宮|version=94116751|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[sn:Kin-gū]] [[ja:金武宮]] [[en:Kin-gū]] [[zh:%E9%87%91%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%AE]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:金武町の建築物]] [[Category:金武町の歴史]] {{Commons category|Kin-gū}} [[zh:金武宮]] {{Commons category|Kin-gū}} [[zh:金武宮]]
